title: Build a REST API — Backward
description: Start from a deployed endpoint and reverse-engineer how APIs work. From Express routes to HTTP semantics, middleware chains, authentication, error handling, CORS, and the TCP layer underneath. Sixteen steps, zero magic.

content.scenario: You have a working API: `GET /api/users` returns a JSON list. Your task: make `POST /api/users` create a new user and return it with status 201. Here is the incomplete server.
content.hint: Use app.post() with the same path. Access the body via req.body.name (express.json() is already set up). Use res.status(201).json(newUser).

content.theory:
## HTTP Methods

HTTP defines **methods** (verbs) that communicate the intent of a request:

| Method | CRUD | Idempotent | Safe | Body |
|--------|------|------------|------|------|
| GET | Read | Yes | Yes | No |
| POST | Create | No | No | Yes |
| PUT | Replace | Yes | No | Yes |
| PATCH | Update | No | No | Yes |
| DELETE | Remove | Yes | No | Optional |

### Why Semantics Matter

The method isn't just a label — it affects real behavior:

- **Caching**: Browsers and CDNs cache GET responses automatically. They never cache POST.
- **Retries**: If a GET request fails, clients can safely retry (idempotent). A POST might create duplicates.
- **Preflight**: Browsers send an OPTIONS request before non-simple POST/PUT/DELETE requests (CORS).
- **Bookmarking**: GET requests can be bookmarked and shared. POST can't.

### Idempotency

An idempotent method produces the same result whether called once or 100 times:

```
PUT /api/users/42 {name: 'Bob'}  → always sets name to Bob
DELETE /api/users/42             → always ensures user 42 is gone
POST /api/users {name: 'Bob'}    → creates a NEW user each time
```

This is critical for reliability. If a network timeout occurs during PUT, the client can safely retry.
content.keyInsight: HTTP methods are semantic contracts. GET reads, POST creates. The method determines caching, retry safety, and browser behavior — not just server routing.

expandableSections.title: HEAD and OPTIONS — the forgotten methods
expandableSections.content:
**HEAD** is identical to GET but returns only headers (no body). Used to check if a resource exists or get its Content-Length before downloading.

**OPTIONS** returns the allowed methods for a URL and CORS headers. Browsers send it automatically before cross-origin POST/PUT/DELETE (preflight check).

content.scenario: Your POST handler receives the request but `req.body` is `undefined`. The client sends JSON with Content-Type: application/json. The server crashes with 'Cannot read property name of undefined'. Something is missing before your route.
content.hint: Express does NOT parse request bodies by default. You need middleware that reads the raw bytes from the TCP stream, parses JSON, and attaches it to req.body.

content.theory:
## Middleware

Middleware functions execute **between** request arrival and response. They form a pipeline:

```
Request → [Logger] → [Auth] → [JSON Parser] → [Route Handler] → Response
                 ↘ (can short-circuit: 401)
```

Each middleware receives `(req, res, next)`:

```javascript
function logger(req, res, next) {
  console.log(`${req.method} ${req.url}`);
  next(); // Pass to next middleware
}

function auth(req, res, next) {
  if (!req.headers.authorization) {
    return res.status(401).json({ error: 'No token' }); // Short-circuit
  }
  next(); // Continue pipeline
}

app.use(logger);           // Runs on ALL requests
app.use('/api', auth);     // Runs only on /api/* routes
app.use(express.json());   // Parses JSON bodies
```

### What express.json() Actually Does

1. Check Content-Type header (application/json?)
2. Read raw bytes from the TCP stream (req is a Readable stream)
3. Buffer the entire body into memory
4. Call `JSON.parse()` on the buffer
5. Attach the result to `req.body`
6. Call `next()`

Without it, the body bytes are never read from the stream — they're discarded.

### Order Matters

Middleware runs in **registration order**. If you register `express.json()` AFTER your route, the route runs first and finds `req.body` undefined.
content.keyInsight: Middleware is a pipeline of functions between request and response. Each can transform the request (add parsed body, verify auth) or short-circuit (reject with 401). Order of registration matters.

expandableSections.title: Common middleware patterns
expandableSections.content:
| Middleware | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| cors() | Add CORS headers | `app.use(cors())` |
| helmet() | Security headers | `app.use(helmet())` |
| morgan() | HTTP request logging | `app.use(morgan('combined'))` |
| compression() | gzip responses | `app.use(compression())` |
| rateLimit() | Throttle requests | `app.use(rateLimit({max: 100}))` |

All follow the same `(req, res, next)` contract.

expandableSections.title: Error-handling middleware

steps.title: Dynamic Routes — Get User by ID

content.scenario: The API needs individual user lookups: `GET /api/users/1` should return the user with id 1. Return 404 if not found. Add the route with a dynamic parameter.
content.hint: Express uses :paramName in the path. Access via req.params.id. Remember it's a string — parseInt() it. Return res.status(404).json({error: 'Not found'}) when missing.

content.theory:
## URL Routing

A router maps **URL patterns** to **handler functions**. When a request arrives:

1. Parse the URL path from the HTTP request line
2. Match against registered patterns (in registration order)
3. Extract dynamic segments (`:id` → `req.params.id`)
4. Call the matching handler

```
GET /api/users/42?fields=name,email
    └── path      └── query string

Pattern: /api/users/:id → params = { id: '42' }
Query: req.query = { fields: 'name,email' }
```

### URL Anatomy

```
https://api.example.com:443/api/users/42?fields=name#section
└─ scheme ─┘└── host ───┘└port┘└── path ──┘└─ query ─┘└ fragment
```

- **Scheme**: Protocol (https = HTTP + TLS encryption)
- **Host**: DNS lookup → IP address
- **Port**: Which process on the server (443 = default HTTPS)
- **Path**: Resource location (router matches this)
- **Query**: Parameters (filtering, pagination)
- **Fragment**: Client-side only (never sent to server)

### Route Priority

Express matches routes in **definition order**. Specific routes should come before generic ones:

```javascript
// WRONG: /api/users/:id matches '/api/users/search' first!
app.get('/api/users/:id', getUser);
app.get('/api/users/search', searchUsers);  // Never reached

// RIGHT: specific route first
app.get('/api/users/search', searchUsers);
app.get('/api/users/:id', getUser);
```
content.keyInsight: Routing maps URL patterns to code. Dynamic segments (:id) extract values from the URL. Routes match in definition order — specific patterns before generic ones.

expandableSections.title: Router modules for large apps

content.scenario: Your DELETE endpoint works but returns 200 with a JSON body. The API consumer (a mobile team) complains that their HTTP library expects 204 for successful deletions. Fix the semantics: 204 No Content for success, 404 if user doesn't exist.
content.hint: 204 means 'success, no body to return'. 404 means 'resource not found'. Use res.status(204).end() and res.status(404).json({error: 'Not found'}).

content.theory:
## HTTP Status Codes

Status codes are a **communication protocol** between server and client:

### The Five Categories

| Range | Meaning | Key Codes |
|-------|---------|----------|
| 1xx | Informational | 101 Switching Protocols (WebSocket upgrade) |
| 2xx | Success | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| 3xx | Redirection | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
| 4xx | Client Error | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable, 429 Too Many Requests |
| 5xx | Server Error | 500 Internal Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Unavailable |

### Choosing the Right Code

```
Created a resource?        → 201 + Location header
Deleted successfully?      → 204 (no body)
Bad input from client?     → 400 (malformed) or 422 (valid JSON, invalid data)
Not authenticated?         → 401 (who are you?)
Authenticated but denied?  → 403 (you can't do this)
Resource doesn't exist?    → 404
Conflict (duplicate email)? → 409
Rate limited?              → 429 + Retry-After header
Unexpected server crash?   → 500
```

### Why It Matters

- **401 vs 403**: 401 means 'send credentials'. 403 means 'your credentials are valid, but you don't have permission.'
- **400 vs 422**: 400 means the JSON is malformed. 422 means the JSON is valid but the data is wrong (email already exists).
- **429 + Retry-After**: Clients and load balancers can throttle and retry automatically.
- **304 Not Modified**: Browser uses cached version — saves bandwidth and latency.
content.keyInsight: Status codes are machine-readable semantics. Browsers, caches, proxies, and client libraries make automatic decisions based on them. 200-for-everything is an anti-pattern that breaks these mechanisms.

expandableSections.title: The 401 / 403 distinction that most APIs get wrong
expandableSections.content:
Most APIs return 403 for everything auth-related. The correct semantics:

- **401 Unauthorized** (misnomer — should be 'Unauthenticated'): 'I don't know who you are. Send a valid token.'
- **403 Forbidden**: 'I know who you are (valid token), but you don't have permission for this resource.'

Why this matters: OAuth clients will attempt to refresh tokens on 401. On 403, they know re-authenticating won't help.

steps.title: Input Validation — Garbage In, Garbage Out

content.scenario: Users can POST any JSON and it creates a user — even empty objects or objects with random fields. Add validation: require name (string, 2-50 chars) and email (valid format). Return 422 with error details for invalid input.
content.hint: Check typeof, string length, and email format before creating the user. Return res.status(422).json({ errors: [...] }) for invalid input.

content.keyInsight: Never trust client input. Validate types, lengths, formats, and ranges. Return 422 with structured error details. Use schema validation libraries (Zod, Joi) for maintainability.

expandableSections.title: Validation middleware pattern

content.scenario: Your API is public — anyone can create, update, or delete users. Add Bearer token authentication middleware that checks for a valid token in the Authorization header. Protected routes should return 401 without a token.
content.hint: Create a function auth(req, res, next) that checks req.headers.authorization. Apply it as middleware to specific routes: app.post('/api/users', auth, handler).

content.keyInsight: Authentication verifies identity (who), authorization checks permissions (what). Bearer tokens (JWTs) are stateless — the server can verify without a database lookup. Auth middleware protects routes selectively.

expandableSections.title: Sessions vs JWTs — the tradeoffs
expandableSections.content:
**Sessions**: Server stores state in memory/DB. Cookie holds session ID. Can revoke instantly.

**JWTs**: Stateless — server stores nothing. Token is self-contained. Cannot revoke until expiration (without a blacklist).

| Concern | Sessions | JWTs |
|---------|----------|------|
| State | Server-side | Client-side |
| Revocation | Instant | Requires blacklist |
| Scaling | Needs shared store | Stateless |
| Size | Small cookie | Large token |
| Security | CSRF risk | XSS risk (if in localStorage) |

Best practice: Short-lived JWTs (15min) + long-lived refresh tokens (httpOnly cookie).

content.scenario: Your React frontend at localhost:3000 calls the API at localhost:8080. The browser blocks the request: 'CORS policy: No Access-Control-Allow-Origin header'. The same curl command works fine. Fix the server to allow cross-origin requests.
content.hint: Add middleware that sets the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. For development, allow your frontend origin. Also handle OPTIONS preflight requests.

steps.title: CORS — Why Browsers Block and Curl Doesn't

content.theory:
## Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)

### The Same-Origin Policy

Browsers enforce the **Same-Origin Policy**: JavaScript on `origin-a.com` cannot read responses from `origin-b.com`. An origin is `scheme + host + port`:

```
http://localhost:3000  ≠  http://localhost:8080   (different port)
https://app.com        ≠  https://api.app.com     (different host)
http://site.com        ≠  https://site.com         (different scheme)
```

### Why curl works

curl isn't a browser — it doesn't enforce the Same-Origin Policy. CORS is purely a **browser security mechanism** to protect users from malicious JavaScript.

### The CORS Handshake

**Simple requests** (GET with standard headers): Browser sends request, checks response for `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`.

**Preflight requests** (POST with JSON, custom headers): Browser first sends OPTIONS:

```
1. Browser: OPTIONS /api/users
   Origin: http://localhost:3000
   Access-Control-Request-Method: POST
   Access-Control-Request-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization

2. Server: 204
   Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000
   Access-Control-Allow-Methods: POST, GET, PUT, DELETE
   Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
   Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400

3. Browser: (preflight passed) → sends actual POST request
```

### Production Configuration

```javascript
// Never use * with credentials:
app.use(cors({
  origin: ['https://app.example.com', 'https://admin.example.com'],
  methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE'],
  allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization'],
  credentials: true, // Allow cookies
  maxAge: 86400      // Cache preflight for 24h
}));
```
content.keyInsight: CORS is a browser security policy that blocks cross-origin requests by default. The server must explicitly allow specific origins via headers. Curl and servers don't enforce CORS — only browsers do.

expandableSections.title: Why ' * ' (wildcard) is dangerous with credentials
expandableSections.content:
If you set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`, browsers REFUSE to send cookies or Authorization headers. This prevents a malicious site from making authenticated requests to your API with the user's cookies.

For authenticated APIs, you MUST specify the exact origin:
```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://yourdomain.com
```

Never dynamically reflect the Origin header without validation — that defeats the purpose of CORS.

content.scenario: When the database connection fails, your Express app crashes with an unhandled exception and the process exits. Add centralized error handling: catch async errors and return proper 500 responses instead of crashing.
content.hint: Wrap async handlers with try/catch, or create an asyncHandler wrapper. Add a 4-argument error middleware at the END of the middleware chain.

content.theory:
## The Full Request Journey

HTTP rides on **TCP** (Transmission Control Protocol). Here's what happens when a browser fetches your API:

```
Browser
  │
  ├── 1. DNS lookup: api.example.com → 93.184.216.34
  ├── 2. TCP handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)
  ├── 3. TLS handshake (certificate exchange, key agreement)
  ├── 4. HTTP request (text over encrypted TCP):
  │      GET /api/users HTTP/1.1
  │      Host: api.example.com
  │      Authorization: Bearer eyJhbG...
  │      Accept: application/json
  │
  ├── 5. Server processes (middleware → route handler)
  │
  ├── 6. HTTP response (text over encrypted TCP):
  │      HTTP/1.1 200 OK
  │      Content-Type: application/json
  │      [{ "id": 1, "name": "Alice" }]
  │
  └── 7. TCP connection kept alive for future requests (HTTP/1.1 keep-alive)
```

### Ports and app.listen(3000)

A **port** is a 16-bit number (0-65535) that multiplexes applications on one IP address. Think: IP = building address, port = apartment number.

`app.listen(3000)` asks the OS to reserve port 3000 exclusively for this process via a **TCP socket**. That's why `EADDRINUSE` means another process already claimed it.

### TCP Guarantees

| Property | Meaning |
|----------|--------|
| Reliable | Data arrives or you get an error |
| Ordered | Bytes arrive in the sequence sent |
| Connection-oriented | Three-way handshake before data |
| Flow control | Sender slows if receiver is overwhelmed |

### The Layer Model

```
Your Express route handler  (Application — HTTP)
         ↓
     Node.js / OS           (Transport — TCP)
         ↓
     Network card            (Network — IP)
         ↓
     WiFi / Ethernet         (Link — physical)
```

Each layer has its own protocol, headers, and failure modes. HTTP status 503 means your application layer is unavailable. A TCP RST means the connection was forcibly closed. Understanding the layers helps you debug network issues.
content.keyInsight: HTTP is a text protocol riding on TCP. TCP guarantees reliable, ordered delivery via three-way handshake. Ports route data to the right process. Every API call traverses DNS → TCP → TLS → HTTP — understanding each layer makes you a better debugger.

expandableSections.title: HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3
expandableSections.content:
**HTTP/1.1**: Text-based, one request per TCP connection (or pipelining). Keep-alive reuses connections.

**HTTP/2**: Binary framing, multiplexing (many requests over ONE TCP connection), header compression (HPACK), server push.

**HTTP/3**: Runs on QUIC (UDP-based) instead of TCP. Eliminates head-of-line blocking. Faster connection setup (0-RTT). Used by Google, Cloudflare, Meta.

The move from TCP to QUIC fixes TCP's biggest problem: if one packet is lost, ALL streams are blocked until retransmit. QUIC isolates streams independently.

expandableSections.title: Why understanding TCP matters for API design
expandableSections.content:
TCP concepts directly impact API performance:

- **Connection pooling**: Creating TCP connections is expensive (3-way handshake + TLS = ~100ms). HTTP clients reuse connections.
- **Keep-alive**: HTTP/1.1 defaults to persistent connections. `Connection: close` forces a new connection per request.
- **Nagle's algorithm**: TCP buffers small writes to reduce overhead. This can add 200ms latency to small API responses. `socket.setNoDelay(true)` disables it.
- **TCP window size**: Limits throughput on new connections ('slow start'). Streaming large responses may start slow.
